abstract:
Recently DeepSeek R1 has shown that reinforcement learning (RL) can
substantially improve the reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models
(LLMs) through a simple yet effective design. The core of R1 lies in its
rule-based reward formulation, which leverages tasks with deterministic
ground-truth answers to enable precise and stable reward computation. In the
visual domain, we similarly observe that a wide range of visual understanding
tasks are inherently equipped with well-defined ground-truth annotations. This
property makes them naturally compatible with rule-based reward mechanisms.
Motivated by this observation, we investigate the extension of R1-style
reinforcement learning to Vision-Language Models (VLMs), aiming to enhance
their visual reasoning capabilities. To this end, we develop VLM-R1, a
dedicated framework designed to harness RL for improving VLMs' performance on
general vision-language tasks. Using this framework, we further explore the
feasibility of applying RL to visual domain. Experimental results indicate that
the RL-based model not only delivers competitive performance on visual
understanding tasks but also surpasses Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) in
generalization ability. Furthermore, we conduct comprehensive ablation studies
that uncover a series of noteworthy insights, including the presence of reward
hacking in object detection, the emergence of the "OD aha moment", the impact
of training data quality, and the scaling behavior of RL across different model
sizes. Through these analyses, we aim to deepen the understanding of how
reinforcement learning enhances the capabilities of vision-language models, and
we hope our findings and open-source contributions will support continued
progress in the vision-language RL community. Our code and model are available
at this https URL

keyInsights: R1-style reinforcement learning enhances VLM generalization to out-of-domain visual reasoning tasks, moving beyond supervised fine-tuning's tendency for memorization.
Careful reward engineering, exemplified by the 'odLength' reward, is crucial to mitigate 'reward hacking' and align model behavior with true task objectives in visual RL.
RL can foster emergent reasoning behaviors, such as the 'OD aha moment' where the VLM spontaneously adopts a two-step thinking process for complex object detection.
originalProblem: Large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) often struggle with generalization and precise visual reasoning, despite their size, frequently underperforming specialized vision models on tasks like Referring Expression Comprehension (REC) and Open-Vocabulary Object Detection (OVD).
Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) of VLMs frequently leads to memorization of training data rather than true generalization of reasoning capabilities to novel visual scenarios.
The nascent field of applying reinforcement learning (RL) to VLMs lacked a dedicated, flexible, and comprehensive framework for experimentation and in-depth analysis.
results: VLM-R1 achieved substantial generalization improvements on out-of-domain REC tasks (e.g., 63.14% accuracy on LISA-Grounding for the 3B model, surpassing SFT's 54.82%).

The RL model significantly outperformed SFT on OVD benchmarks (e.g., 21.1% mAP on COCO_filtered and 31.01 nms-AP on OVDEval for the 3B model), showing gains in complex categories like Position and Relationship detection.
The introduced 'odLength' reward effectively prevented reward hacking in OVD, leading to more precise outputs and an emergent 'OD aha moment' where the model explicitly plans its detections.
solution: Introduces VLM-R1, an open-source framework extending DeepSeek R1's rule-based reinforcement learning approach to Vision-Language Models, built for modularity and scalability.

Employs the Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) algorithm with custom, rule-based reward functions designed for precise visual tasks like Referring Expression Comprehension (REC) and Open-Vocabulary Object Detection (OVD).
Developed an 'odLength' reward for OVD, which penalizes redundant predictions to counteract reward hacking and encourage precise object localization and quantity prediction.
paper_summary.summary: VLM-R1 introduces an open-source framework that applies rule-based reinforcement learning to Vision-Language Models (VLMs), enhancing their visual reasoning and generalization abilities on tasks like Referring Expression Comprehension and Open-Vocabulary Object Detection. The approach demonstrates improved out-of-domain performance compared to supervised fine-tuning and showcases emergent reasoning behaviors.

title: VLM-R1: A Stable and Generalizable R1-style Large Vision-Language Model

abstract: Inspired by DeepSeek-R1's success in eliciting reasoning abilities through rule-based reinforcement learning (RL), we introduce Video-R1 as the first attempt to systematically explore the R1 paradigm for incentivizing video reasoning within multimodal large language models (MLLMs). However, directly applying RL training with the GRPO algorithm to video reasoning presents two primary challenges: (i) a lack of temporal modeling for video reasoning, and (ii) the scarcity of high-quality video-reasoning data. To address these issues, we first propose the T-GRPO algorithm, which encourages models to utilize temporal information in videos for reasoning. Additionally, instead of relying solely on video data, we incorporate high-quality image-reasoning data into the training process. We have constructed two datasets: Video-R1-CoT-165k for SFT cold start and Video-R1-260k for RL training, both comprising image and video data. Experimental results demonstrate that Video-R1 achieves significant improvements on video reasoning benchmarks such as VideoMMMU and VSI-Bench, as well as on general video benchmarks including MVBench and TempCompass, etc. Notably, Video-R1-7B attains a 37.1% accuracy on video spatial reasoning benchmark VSI-bench, surpassing the commercial proprietary model GPT-4o. All code, models, and data are released in: this https URL.

keyInsights: Reinforcement Learning (RL) with a temporal-aware algorithm (T-GRPO) is crucial for unlocking generalized and robust video reasoning in MLLMs, offering performance improvements over SFT-only approaches.

The explicit incentivization of temporal understanding through a contrastive reward mechanism effectively guides the MLLM to leverage correct temporal order, leading to deeper temporal reasoning rather than relying on superficial cues.
Combining image-based reasoning data with video data during training provides a robust foundation of general reasoning skills that significantly boosts performance on complex video reasoning tasks.
originalProblem: Existing MLLMs primarily excel at video perception tasks but lack robust, explicit temporal reasoning abilities to understand dynamic events and relationships over time.
Current reinforcement learning (RL) algorithms for reasoning, designed for text, do not inherently provide reward signals that encourage temporal understanding in videos.
A scarcity of high-quality, complex video reasoning datasets hinders the effective training and generalization of MLLMs for sophisticated video-based reasoning tasks.
results: Video-R1 consistently and significantly outperformed previous state-of-the-art MLLMs across six challenging video benchmarks, with Video-R1-7B (64 frames) achieving 37.1% accuracy on VSI-Bench, surpassing GPT-4o (34.0%).
Reinforcement learning, even after only 1k steps, yielded significant performance improvements, demonstrating its effectiveness in generalizing video reasoning capabilities beyond SFT.

Ablation studies confirmed the critical role of both the hybrid image-video data for general reasoning and the T-GRPO algorithm for explicit temporal understanding, as removing either component led to noticeable performance drops.
solution: Constructed two comprehensive datasets, Video-R1-CoT-165k (for SFT) and Video-R1-260k (for RL), incorporating a hybrid of high-quality image and video reasoning data to build foundational and temporal reasoning skills.

Introduced Temporal Group Relative Policy Optimization (T-GRPO), an RL algorithm that explicitly incentivizes temporal reasoning using a contrastive reward mechanism based on comparing responses to ordered versus randomly shuffled video frames.

Implemented a two-stage training strategy: an initial Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) cold start on Video-R1-CoT-165k, followed by RL training with T-GRPO on Video-R1-260k, enhanced with a length-based reward for response coherence.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers introduced Video-R1, the first framework to apply a rule-based reinforcement learning paradigm for enhancing temporal reasoning capabilities in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) for video. Video-R1, leveraging a new temporal-aware RL algorithm and dedicated video reasoning datasets, consistently outperforms previous state-of-the-art models, achieving 37.1% accuracy on VSI-Bench, surpassing GPT-4o's 34.0%.

title: Video-R1: Reinforcing Video Reasoning in MLLMs

abstract:
This work revisits the dominant supervised fine-tuning (SFT) then
reinforcement learning (RL) paradigm for training Large Vision-Language Models
(LVLMs), and reveals a key finding: SFT can significantly undermine subsequent
RL by inducing ``pseudo reasoning paths'' imitated from expert models. While
these paths may resemble the native reasoning paths of RL models, they often
involve prolonged, hesitant, less informative steps, and incorrect reasoning.
To systematically study this effect, we introduce VLAA-Thinking, a new
multimodal dataset designed to support reasoning in LVLMs. Constructed via a
six-step pipeline involving captioning, reasoning distillation, answer rewrite
and verification, VLAA-Thinking comprises high-quality, step-by-step visual
reasoning traces for SFT, along with a more challenging RL split from the same
data source. Using this dataset, we conduct extensive experiments comparing
SFT, RL and their combinations. Results show that while SFT helps models learn
reasoning formats, it often locks aligned models into imitative, rigid
reasoning modes that impede further learning. In contrast, building on the
Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with a novel mixed reward module
integrating both perception and cognition signals, our RL approach fosters more
genuine, adaptive reasoning behavior. Notably, our model VLAA-Thinker, based on
Qwen2.5VL 3B, achieves top-1 performance on Open LMM Reasoning Leaderboard
(this https URL)
among 4B scale LVLMs, surpassing the previous state-of-the-art by 1.8%. We hope
our findings provide valuable insights in developing reasoning-capable LVLMs
and can inform future research in this area.

keyInsights: SFT can induce "pseudo reasoning paths" that hinder genuine reasoning development
Increasing SFT data volume or model size does not mitigate adverse effects
Direct RL training through GRPO consistently outperforms SFT-based approaches
originalProblem: Traditional "SFT then RL" training paradigm's effectiveness for vision-language models remains unexamined
Lack of understanding about how SFT and RL distinctly contribute to multimodal reasoning capabilities
results: VLAA-Thinker-Qwen2.5VL-3B achieves top performance among 4B-scale models on Open LMM Reasoning Leaderboard
Models trained directly with GRPO show emergence of authentic self-reflective reasoning
SFT followed by GRPO performs worse than GRPO alone, challenging the conventional training paradigm
solution: Created VLAA-Thinking dataset with carefully curated SFT and RL splits based on presence of self-reflective reasoning
Implemented mixed reward GRPO framework combining rule-based and open-ended rewards
Conducted systematic comparison of SFT, RL, and combined approaches across multiple model scales
paper_summary.summary: A comparative study examines the effectiveness of Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) versus Reinforcement Learning (RL) for training reasoning-capable vision-language models, revealing that SFT can impede subsequent RL performance by inducing superficial reasoning patterns, while direct RL training through Group Relative Policy Optimization achieves state-of-the-art performance on multimodal reasoning benchmarks.

title: SFT or RL? An Early Investigation into Training R1-Like Reasoning Large  Vision-Language Models

abstract: Today's most advanced vision-language models (VLMs) remain proprietary. The strongest open-weight models rely heavily on synthetic data from proprietary VLMs to achieve good performance, effectively distilling these closed VLMs into open ones. As a result, the community has been missing foundational knowledge about how to build performant VLMs from scratch. We present Molmo, a new family of VLMs that are state-of-the-art in their class of openness. Our key contribution is a collection of new datasets called PixMo, including a dataset of highly detailed image captions for pre-training, a free-form image Q&A dataset for fine-tuning, and an innovative 2D pointing dataset, all collected without the use of external VLMs. The success of our approach relies on careful modeling choices, a well-tuned training pipeline, and, most critically, the quality of our newly collected datasets. Our best-in-class 72B model not only outperforms others in the class of open weight and data models, but also outperforms larger proprietary models including Claude 3.5 Sonnet, and Gemini 1.5 Pro and Flash, second only to GPT-4o based on both academic benchmarks and on a large human evaluation. Our model weights, new datasets, and source code are available at this https URL.

keyInsights: High-quality human-annotated data, collected without reliance on proprietary VLMs, performs comparably to data generated by powerful commercial models for VLM training.
Architectural choices like overlapping multi-crops for vision input and text-only dropout during pre-training significantly improve VLM performance.
Targeted, VLM-independent synthetic data can effectively imbue models with robust, specialized skills, as demonstrated by leading performance in clock reading and counting.
originalProblem: Many open-weight Vision-Language Models (VLMs) rely heavily on synthetic data generated by proprietary VLMs, hindering true scientific understanding and reproducibility of VLM development.
Collecting large-scale, high-quality multimodal training data is expensive and difficult, often leading to reliance on less transparent or less effective methods.
Lack of truly open, foundational VLM resources creates barriers to independent research and development outside of large tech companies.
results: Molmo-72B achieves state-of-the-art academic benchmark scores among all tested models and ranks second in human preference (Elo score) just behind GPT-4o.
Molmo models significantly outperform other VLMs, including proprietary ones, on counting tasks (e.g., CountBenchQA) and an in-the-wild clock reading benchmark.
The most efficient model, MolmoE-1B, based on a 1B-7B mixture-of-experts LLM, approaches the performance of GPT-4V on academic benchmarks and human preference.
solution: Develops Molmo, a family of state-of-the-art open-weight VLMs with a streamlined architecture featuring a pre-trained vision encoder, an LLM, and an optimized connector using overlapping multi-crop vision input.

Introduces PixMo, a novel suite of seven multimodal datasets collected entirely without relying on proprietary or other external VLMs, utilizing innovative methods like oral descriptions for dense captions and interactive human-in-the-loop editing for Q&A.

Employs a simplified two-stage training pipeline (pre-training on dense captions, fine-tuning on a mix of all PixMo and academic datasets) with specific optimizations like text-only dropout and efficient multi-annotation processing.
paper_summary.summary: This work introduces Molmo, a family of state-of-the-art open-weight Vision-Language Models (VLMs), and PixMo, a comprehensive suite of VLM-independent training datasets. Molmo-72B achieves leading performance on academic benchmarks and human preference evaluations, demonstrating that high-performing VLMs can be built entirely from truly open data and models without relying on proprietary systems for data generation.

title: Molmo and PixMo: Open Weights and Open Data for State-of-the-Art Vision-Language Models

abstract: We introduce OpenVLThinker, one of the first open-source large vision-language models (LVLMs) to exhibit sophisticated chain-of-thought reasoning, achieving notable performance gains on challenging visual reasoning tasks. While text-based reasoning models (e.g., Deepseek R1) show promising results in text-only tasks, distilling their reasoning into LVLMs via supervised fine-tuning (SFT) often results in performance degradation due to imprecise visual grounding. Conversely, purely reinforcement learning (RL)-based methods face a large search space, hindering the emergence of reflective behaviors in smaller models (e.g., 7B LVLMs). Surprisingly, alternating between SFT and RL ultimately results in significant performance improvements after a few iterations. Our analysis reveals that the base model rarely exhibits reasoning behaviors initially, but SFT effectively surfaces these latent actions and narrows the RL search space, accelerating the development of reasoning capabilities. Each subsequent RL stage further refines the model's reasoning skills, producing higher-quality SFT data for continued self-improvement. OpenVLThinker-7B consistently advances performance across six benchmarks demanding mathematical and general reasoning, notably improving MathVista by 3.8%, EMMA by 2.4%, and HallusionBench by 1.6%. Beyond demonstrating the synergy between SFT and RL for complex reasoning tasks, our findings provide early evidence towards achieving R1-style reasoning in multimodal contexts. The code, model and data are held at this https URL.

keyInsights: The iterative SFT-RL cycle synergistically fosters complex reasoning, where initial SFT acts as an 'action highlighting' mechanism to surface reasoning behaviors and narrow the RL search space.

RL refines these reasoning actions and improves generalization from the SFT-provided foundation, playing a crucial role in enhancing overall accuracy and generating better SFT data for subsequent iterations.

Robust complex reasoning can be instilled in LVLMs with significantly less training data (using only 12K examples per iteration) compared to concurrent single-iteration methods that require 10x more data.
originalProblem: Open-source Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) typically lack the sophisticated multi-step reasoning capabilities (e.g., planning, reflection, verification) observed in proprietary multimodal models or text-only R1 models.
Distilling reasoning directly from text-only R1 models into LVLMs through supervised fine-tuning (SFT) often leads to performance degradation due to imprecise visual grounding.

Applying pure reinforcement learning (RL) from scratch to smaller LVLMs (e.g., 7B parameters) for reasoning is inefficient due to the prohibitively large search space for reflective behaviors to emerge.
results: OpenVLThinker-7B achieves state-of-the-art performance among open-source LVLMs across six challenging benchmarks, with average accuracy of 47.9% and specific improvements such as 3.8% on MathVista and 2.4% on EMMA.
The model performs competitively with larger proprietary models like GPT-4o (47.3% average) and Claude-3.5-Sonnet (48.4% average) on Math and Perception tasks.

Empirical analysis validates that while initial SFT might cause a temporary performance drop, the iterative SFT-RL cycle consistently yields significant gains, confirming the efficacy of the proposed self-improvement paradigm.
solution: An iterative self-improvement framework alternates between Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and Reinforcement Learning (RL) using Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO).
SFT datasets are curated by using the base LVLM for captioning, feeding captions to a text-based R1 model for CoT generation, and then self-generating higher-quality CoTs in subsequent iterations.
A two-stage, source-based curriculum RL strategy trains the model first on medium-difficulty data and then on hard-difficulty data to optimize learning efficiency and maximize performance.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at UCLA developed OpenVLThinker, an open-source large vision-language model, leveraging an iterative supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning cycle to achieve complex, multi-step reasoning. This model demonstrates competitive performance with proprietary LVLMs and surpasses other open-source models on challenging benchmarks, showing data efficiency with 10x less data.

title: OpenVLThinker: Complex Vision-Language Reasoning via Iterative SFT-RL Cycles

abstract: We present GLM-4.1V-Thinking, GLM-4.5V, and GLM-4.6V, a family of vision-language models (VLMs) designed to advance general-purpose multimodal understanding and reasoning. In this report, we share our key findings in the development of the reasoning-centric training framework. We first develop a capable vision foundation model with significant potential through large-scale pre-training, which arguably sets the upper bound for the final performance. We then propose Reinforcement Learning with Curriculum Sampling (RLCS) to unlock the full potential of the model, leading to comprehensive capability enhancement across a diverse range of tasks, including STEM problem solving, video understanding, content recognition, coding, grounding, GUI-based agents, and long document interpretation. In a comprehensive evaluation across 42 public benchmarks, GLM-4.5V achieves state-of-the-art performance on nearly all tasks among open-source models of similar size, and demonstrates competitive or even superior results compared to closed-source models such as Gemini-2.5-Flash on challenging tasks including Coding and GUI Agents. Meanwhile, the smaller GLM-4.1V-9B-Thinking remains highly competitive-achieving superior results to the much larger Qwen2.5-VL-72B on 29 benchmarks. We open-source both GLM-4.1V-9B-Thinking and GLM-4.5V. We further introduce the GLM-4.6V series, open-source multimodal models with native tool use and a 128K context window. A brief overview is available at this https URL. Code, models and more information are released at this https URL.

keyInsights: Multi-domain reinforcement learning promotes robust cross-domain generalization and mutual facilitation, where training on one domain positively impacts performance in others.
Dynamically selecting informative rollout problems through Reinforcement Learning with Curriculum Sampling (RLCS) is critical for maximizing learning gains and overall efficiency.
A precise and hack-resistant multi-domain reward system is essential for the stability and effectiveness of large-scale RL training across diverse multimodal tasks.
originalProblem: Existing Vision-Language Models (VLMs) often specialized in narrow domains, lacking general-purpose reasoning capabilities for complex tasks.
There was a need for VLMs capable of advanced multimodal reasoning in areas such as STEM problem-solving, autonomous GUI agents, coding from designs, and interpreting long documents.

Unlocking the full potential of strong vision foundation models required sophisticated and scalable reinforcement learning (RL) techniques, which were primarily explored for large language models (LLMs).
results: GLM-4.5V achieved state-of-the-art performance on nearly all 42 public benchmarks among open-source models of comparable size, outperforming models like Step-3 (321B-A38B) and Qwen-2.5-VL-72B.
The model demonstrated competitive or superior results compared to closed-source models such as Gemini-2.5-Flash on challenging tasks including VLM Coding and GUI Agents.
The smaller GLM-4.1V-9B-Thinking model achieved superior results to the much larger Qwen2.5-VL-72B on 29 out of 42 benchmarks, highlighting an excellent balance of performance and efficiency.
solution: A three-stage, reasoning-centric training framework was employed, including vision foundation model pre-training, supervised fine-tuning (SFT), and reinforcement learning (RL).
Pre-training involved a unified architecture with an AIMv2-Huge vision encoder and GLM-4-9B-0414 or GLM-4.5-Air LLMs, supporting arbitrary resolutions and diverse multimodal data.

The core Reinforcement Learning stage combined Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) and Human Feedback (RLHF) with a multi-domain reward system and Reinforcement Learning with Curriculum Sampling (RLCS) for efficiency and stability.
paper_summary.summary: The GLM-V Team from Zhipu AI and Tsinghua University developed GLM-4.5V and GLM-4.1V-Thinking, a family of vision-language models designed for versatile multimodal reasoning. These models, trained with a scalable reinforcement learning framework, achieve state-of-the-art performance across 42 diverse public benchmarks, including complex STEM problem-solving, GUI agents, and VLM coding, while maintaining competitive results against closed-source models.

title: GLM-4.5V and GLM-4.1V-Thinking: Towards Versatile Multimodal Reasoning with Scalable Reinforcement Learning

abstract:
Reinforcement learning (RL) has recently shown strong potential in improving
the reasoning capabilities of large language models and is now being actively
extended to vision-language models (VLMs). However, existing RL applications in
VLMs often rely on heavily engineered frameworks that hinder reproducibility
and accessibility, while lacking standardized evaluation protocols, making it
difficult to compare results or interpret training dynamics. This work
introduces a transparent, from-scratch framework for RL in VLMs, offering a
minimal yet functional four-step pipeline validated across multiple models and
datasets. In addition, a standardized evaluation scheme is proposed to assess
training dynamics and reflective behaviors. Extensive experiments on visual
reasoning tasks uncover key empirical findings: response length is sensitive to
random seeds, reflection correlates with output length, and RL consistently
outperforms supervised fine-tuning (SFT) in generalization, even with
high-quality data. These findings, together with the proposed framework, aim to
establish a reproducible baseline and support broader engagement in RL-based
VLM research.
title: Rethinking RL Scaling for Vision Language Models: A Transparent,  From-Scratch Framework and Comprehensive Evaluation Scheme

abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) has recently achieved remarkable success in eliciting visual reasoning within Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs). However, existing approaches typically train separate models for different tasks and treat image and video reasoning as disjoint domains. This results in limited scalability toward a multimodal reasoning generalist, which restricts practical versatility and hinders potential knowledge sharing across tasks and modalities. To this end, we propose OneThinker, an all-in-one reasoning model that unifies image and video understanding across diverse fundamental visual tasks, including question answering, captioning, spatial and temporal grounding, tracking, and segmentation. To achieve this, we construct the OneThinker-600k training corpus covering all these tasks and employ commercial models for CoT annotation, resulting in OneThinker-SFT-340k for SFT cold start. Furthermore, we propose EMA-GRPO to handle reward heterogeneity in multi-task RL by tracking task-wise moving averages of reward standard deviations for balanced optimization. Extensive experiments on diverse visual benchmarks show that OneThinker delivers strong performance on 31 benchmarks, across 10 fundamental visual understanding tasks. Moreover, it exhibits effective knowledge transfer between certain tasks and preliminary zero-shot generalization ability, marking a step toward a unified multimodal reasoning generalist. All code, model, and data are released.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

keyInsights: A single multimodal large language model can successfully unify a broad spectrum of visual reasoning tasks across both static images and dynamic videos, moving beyond specialized models.
Joint training on diverse image and video tasks facilitates significant cross-task and cross-modal knowledge transfer, leading to more robust and generalizable reasoning abilities.
The proposed EMA-GRPO algorithm effectively stabilizes and balances optimization in multi-task, multi-modal reinforcement learning environments by handling heterogeneous reward characteristics.
originalProblem: Existing multimodal large language models (MLLMs) and RL-enhanced models typically specialize in single tasks or a single modality (images or videos), leading to fragmented development and limiting versatility.
There was a lack of a unified multimodal reasoning generalist that could simultaneously handle a wide range of fundamental visual tasks across both image and video modalities.
Applying standard reinforcement learning (RL) algorithms like GRPO to multi-task, multi-modal setups is challenging due to heterogeneous reward characteristics and varying scales across tasks.
results: OneThinker-8B demonstrated consistently strong and often state-of-the-art performance across 31 visual reasoning benchmarks, covering 10 fundamental tasks for both images and videos.

Achieved superior results on complex image QA (e.g., 70.6% on MMMU, 77.6% on MathVista) and significant advancements in video QA (e.g., 66.2% on VideoMMMU, 79.2% on LongVideo-Reason), outperforming strong open-source competitors.

Ablation studies confirmed that EMA-GRPO and RL training consistently improved performance over SFT-only and other GRPO variants, validating the approach's effectiveness and showing preliminary zero-shot generalization on unseen tasks.
solution: Introduced OneThinker, an 'all-in-one' multimodal large language model built upon Qwen3-VL-Instruct-8B, designed to process both image and video inputs for diverse visual understanding tasks.
Curated large-scale multimodal datasets, OneThinker-600k (diverse visual tasks) and OneThinker-SFT-340k (with Chain-of-Thought annotations), to enable comprehensive and unified training.

Developed Exponential Moving Average Group Relative Policy Optimization (EMA-GRPO), an RL algorithm that uses task-wise EMA of reward moments to adaptively normalize advantages, addressing intra-task and inter-task reward imbalances.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from MMLab, CUHK and Meituan developed OneThinker, a unified multimodal large language model capable of diverse visual understanding tasks across images and videos. This model utilizes an EMA-GRPO algorithm to achieve robust performance across 31 benchmarks, setting new state-of-the-art results for many tasks.

title: OneThinker: All-in-one Reasoning Model for Image and Video

abstract: Chain-of-thought reasoning has significantly improved the performance of Large Language Models (LLMs) across various domains. However, this reasoning process has been confined exclusively to textual space, limiting its effectiveness in visually intensive tasks. To address this limitation, we introduce the concept of reasoning in the pixel-space. Within this novel framework, Vision-Language Models (VLMs) are equipped with a suite of visual reasoning operations, such as zoom-in and select-frame. These operations enable VLMs to directly inspect, interrogate, and infer from visual evidences, thereby enhancing reasoning fidelity for visual tasks. Cultivating such pixel-space reasoning capabilities in VLMs presents notable challenges, including the model's initially imbalanced competence and its reluctance to adopt the newly introduced pixel-space operations. We address these challenges through a two-phase training approach. The first phase employs instruction tuning on synthesized reasoning traces to familiarize the model with the novel visual operations. Following this, a reinforcement learning (RL) phase leverages a curiosity-driven reward scheme to balance exploration between pixel-space reasoning and textual reasoning. With these visual operations, VLMs can interact with complex visual inputs, such as information-rich images or videos to proactively gather necessary information. We demonstrate that this approach significantly improves VLM performance across diverse visual reasoning benchmarks. Our 7B model, \model, achieves 84\% on V* bench, 74\% on TallyQA-Complex, and 84\% on InfographicsVQA, marking the highest accuracy achieved by any open-source model to date. These results highlight the importance of pixel-space reasoning and the effectiveness of our framework.

keyInsights: The 'textual reasoning bottleneck' prevents current VLMs from fully exploiting rich visual information, hindering performance on visually intensive tasks.
A 'learning trap' exists where nascent skills like pixel-space reasoning are overlooked by models favoring established, stronger textual reasoning, requiring specific incentives for cultivation.

Combining warm-start instruction tuning, including self-correction trajectories, with curiosity-driven reinforcement learning is critical for developing robust and effective interactive visual reasoning.
originalProblem: Current Vision-Language Models (VLMs) struggle to capture fine-grained visual details due to their reasoning process being confined exclusively to textual space.
Existing VLMs lack the ability to directly interact with visual inputs (e.g., zoom, select frames) during complex reasoning, limiting their capacity for deep visual inference.
The 'textual reasoning bottleneck' leads to potential loss of crucial visual information when translating visual observations into text tokens for intermediate steps.
results: Pixel-Reasoner, a 7B parameter model, achieved state-of-the-art accuracy across four multimodal benchmarks: 84.3% on V* Bench, 73.8% on TallyQA-Complex, 67.8% on MVBench, and 84.0% (ANLS) on InfographicsVQA.

The model outperformed larger open-source models (e.g., 27B Gemma3) and demonstrated performance comparable to or superior to leading proprietary models, including a 5.1 percentage point lead over Gemini-2.5-Pro on V* Bench.

Reinforcement learning, particularly with the curiosity bonus, was essential for cultivating robust pixel-space reasoning, as instruction tuning alone initially led to a decrease in performance on several benchmarks.
solution: Introduces pixel-space reasoning, where the VLM policy generates sequences of either textual thinking or visual operations (`zoom-in`, `select-frame`) to directly manipulate visual inputs.
Employs a two-phase post-training approach: warm-start instruction tuning for foundational understanding and error-induced self-correction capabilities.

Utilizes curiosity-driven reinforcement learning with a dynamic reward scheme to overcome the 'learning trap' by incentivizing exploration and mastery of pixel-space operations when usage is historically low.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from the University of Waterloo, HKUST, and Vector Institute developed Pixel Reasoner, a system that enables Vision-Language Models to perform reasoning directly in pixel space using visual operations like zoom-in and select-frame. This approach achieved state-of-the-art accuracy on four multimodal benchmarks, surpassing larger open-source and several proprietary models.

title: Pixel Reasoner: Incentivizing Pixel-Space Reasoning with Curiosity-Driven Reinforcement Learning

abstract:
We open-source MiMo-VL-7B-SFT and MiMo-VL-7B-RL, two powerful vision-language
models delivering state-of-the-art performance in both general visual
understanding and multimodal reasoning. MiMo-VL-7B-RL outperforms Qwen2.5-VL-7B
on 35 out of 40 evaluated tasks, and scores 59.4 on OlympiadBench, surpassing
models with up to 78B parameters. For GUI grounding applications, it sets a new
standard with 56.1 on OSWorld-G, even outperforming specialized models such as
UI-TARS. Our training combines four-stage pre-training (2.4 trillion tokens)
with Mixed On-policy Reinforcement Learning (MORL) integrating diverse reward
signals. We identify the importance of incorporating high-quality reasoning
data with long Chain-of-Thought into pre-training stages, and the benefits of
mixed RL despite challenges in simultaneous multi-domain optimization. We also
contribute a comprehensive evaluation suite covering 50+ tasks to promote
reproducibility and advance the field. The model checkpoints and full
evaluation suite are available at this https URL
authors: et al. (6 additional authors not shown)

full_authors.full_name: et al. (6 additional authors not shown)

keyInsights: Incorporating reasoning data with long Chain-of-Thought directly into late pre-training stages yields substantial performance gains (+9 MMMU, +14 OSWorld-G, +16 OlympiadBench) compared to post-training only approaches
On-policy reinforcement learning variants demonstrate superior scaling properties and training stability compared to vanilla GRPO, showing continuous improvement without early saturation
Task interference remains a significant challenge in mixed RL objectives, with disparities in response length requirements across different task types limiting unified optimization
Compact 7B parameter models can achieve competitive performance with much larger models through sophisticated training methodologies and high-quality data curation
originalProblem: Existing compact vision-language models lag behind larger proprietary models on complex multimodal reasoning and agentic tasks
Current VLM training approaches primarily incorporate reasoning capabilities through post-training fine-tuning rather than pre-training
Limited availability of comprehensive, standardized evaluation frameworks for fair VLM comparison across diverse tasks
Challenge of integrating multiple reward signals (verifiable rewards and human feedback) in reinforcement learning for VLMs
results: MiMo-VL-7B-RL achieves 59.4 on OlympiadBench, surpassing models up to 72B parameters and setting new standards for multimodal reasoning
Outperforms specialized GUI models on OSWorld-G (56.1 score) despite being a general-purpose VLM, demonstrating strong agentic capabilities
Maintains excellent text-only performance (95.4% MATH500, 85.1% DROP) while excelling across diverse multimodal tasks including video understanding and document analysis
Achieves highest Elo rating among open-source VLMs in user preference evaluation, approaching proprietary model performance levels
solution: Four-stage pre-training architecture with strategic data progression from basic alignment to complex reasoning integration
Mixed On-policy Reinforcement Learning (MORL) framework combining rule-based verifiable rewards with human feedback through "Reward-as-a-Service" system
Direct incorporation of high-quality synthetic reasoning data with long Chain-of-Thought into Stage 4 pre-training rather than only fine-tuning
Comprehensive open-source evaluation suite covering 50+ multimodal benchmarks with standardized prompts and protocols
paper_summary.summary: Xiaomi's LLM-Core division develops MiMo-VL-7B, a compact vision-language model that achieves state-of-the-art performance across 50+ multimodal benchmarks through a four-stage training process consuming 2.4 trillion tokens and novel Mixed On-policy Reinforcement Learning (MORL) framework, with the resulting MiMo-VL-7B-RL model outperforming Qwen2.5-VL-7B on 35 out of 40 tasks and surpassing much larger models (up to 78B parameters) on complex reasoning benchmarks like OlympiadBench (59.4 score) and GUI grounding tasks like OSWorld-G (56.1 score), while achieving 95.4% on MATH500 and maintaining the highest Elo rating among open-source VLMs in user preference evaluations, accomplished through strategic incorporation of high-quality Chain-of-Thought reasoning data directly into late pre-training stages rather than only post-training fine-tuning.

title: MiMo-VL Technical Report

abstract:
Reinforcement learning (RL) has significantly advanced the reasoning
capabilities of vision-language models (VLMs). However, the use of RL beyond
reasoning tasks remains largely unexplored, especially for perceptionintensive
tasks like object detection and grounding. We propose V-Triune, a Visual Triple
Unified Reinforcement Learning system that enables VLMs to jointly learn visual
reasoning and perception tasks within a single training pipeline. V-Triune
comprises triple complementary components: Sample-Level Data Formatting (to
unify diverse task inputs), Verifier-Level Reward Computation (to deliver
custom rewards via specialized verifiers) , and Source-Level Metric Monitoring
(to diagnose problems at the data-source level). We further introduce a novel
Dynamic IoU reward, which provides adaptive, progressive, and definite feedback
for perception tasks handled by V-Triune. Our approach is instantiated within
off-the-shelf RL training framework using open-source 7B and 32B backbone
models. The resulting model, dubbed Orsta (One RL to See Them All),
demonstrates consistent improvements across both reasoning and perception
tasks. This broad capability is significantly shaped by its training on a
diverse dataset, constructed around four representative visual reasoning tasks
(Math, Puzzle, Chart, and Science) and four visual perception tasks (Grounding,
Detection, Counting, and OCR). Subsequently, Orsta achieves substantial gains
on MEGA-Bench Core, with improvements ranging from +2.1 to an impressive +14.1
across its various 7B and 32B model variants, with performance benefits
extending to a wide range of downstream tasks. These results highlight the
effectiveness and scalability of our unified RL approach for VLMs. The V-Triune
system, along with the Orsta models, is publicly available at
this https URL

keyInsights: Reinforcement Learning in VLMs primarily functions as an alignment strategy, refining decision-making and unlocking latent capabilities of pre-trained models rather than introducing entirely new knowledge.

Freezing the Visual Transformer (ViT) parameters and employing conservative learning rates are critical engineering optimizations for achieving stable and peak performance during VLM RL training, preventing instability.

Training VLMs on a combined corpus of reasoning and perception tasks leads to additive performance gains and transferable alignment signals, indicating synergy between these diverse learning objectives.
originalProblem: Reinforcement Learning (RL) applications for Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have primarily focused on visual reasoning tasks, neglecting perception-intensive tasks like object detection and grounding.

The absence of a unified RL framework makes it challenging to integrate diverse input formats, reward mechanisms, and evaluation metrics needed for jointly training VLMs on both reasoning and perception.
Current RL setups for VLMs lack specialized reward designs and stability measures for perception tasks, leading to issues such as reward ambiguity with fixed IoU thresholds or cold-start problems.
results: Orsta models achieved up to a +14.1% performance increase on the MEGA-Bench Core benchmark compared to their Qwen2.5-VL backbones, with substantial improvements in mathematical and perception-related tasks.

Visual perception capabilities improved significantly, including +7.81 mAP for single-object detection and +3.77 mAP for multi-object detection on COCO, along with +5.3 mAP on OVDEval and new state-of-the-art results on CountBench.
Visual reasoning saw notable gains, with Orsta-7B improving by 4% on MMMU and all Orsta variants achieving over 5% gains on the MathVista benchmark.
solution: Developed V-Triune, a unified RL system with a three-component architecture for Sample-Level Data Formatting, Verifier-Level Reward Computation via an asynchronous client-server, and Source-Level Metric Monitoring.

Introduced a Dynamic IoU reward mechanism for visual perception tasks, which adaptively adjusts the Intersection over Union threshold during training to facilitate stable learning towards high-precision outcomes.

Implemented a comprehensive training recipe featuring frozen Visual Transformer (ViT) parameters, filtering of spurious special tokens, a Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompt pool, and optimized memory management to enhance training stability.
paper_summary.summary: A Visual Triple Unified Reinforcement Learning system, V-Triune, enables Vision-Language Models to simultaneously improve across visual reasoning and perception tasks within a single training pipeline. Models trained using V-Triune, named Orsta, show performance gains of up to +14.1% on the MEGA-Bench Core, along with specific improvements like +7.81 mAP for COCO Detection and over 5% on the MathVista benchmark.

title: One RL to See Them All: Visual Triple Unified Reinforcement Learning

abstract: We introduce SAIL-VL2, an open-suite vision-language foundation model (LVM) for comprehensive multimodal understanding and reasoning. As the successor to SAIL-VL, SAIL-VL2 achieves state-of-the-art performance at the 2B and 8B parameter scales across diverse image and video benchmarks, demonstrating strong capabilities from fine-grained perception to complex reasoning. Its effectiveness is driven by three core innovations. First, a large-scale data curation pipeline with scoring and filtering strategies enhances both quality and distribution across captioning, OCR, QA, and video data, improving training efficiency. Second, a progressive training framework begins with a powerful pre-trained vision encoder (SAIL-ViT), advances through multimodal pre-training, and culminates in a thinking-fusion SFT-RL hybrid paradigm that systematically strengthens model capabilities. Third, architectural advances extend beyond dense LLMs to efficient sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) designs. With these contributions, SAIL-VL2 demonstrates competitive performance across 106 datasets and achieves state-of-the-art results on challenging reasoning benchmarks such as MMMU and MathVista. Furthermore, on the OpenCompass leaderboard, SAIL-VL2-2B ranks first among officially released open-source models under the 4B parameter scale, while serving as an efficient and extensible foundation for the open-source multimodal community.

keyInsights: State-of-the-art vision-language model performance can be achieved efficiently with smaller parameter counts by strategically innovating in data quality, architectural design, and progressive training methodologies.

Systematic and meticulous data curation, including automatic quality filtering, specialized content generation (e.g., chart captions), and synthetic data expansion, is critical for building robust and generalizable multimodal capabilities.

The combination of progressive supervised fine-tuning with reinforcement learning, especially when guided by Chain-of-Thought and sophisticated mixed reward systems, significantly enhances an LVM's ability for complex, multi-step multimodal reasoning.
originalProblem: The prevailing trend of increasingly large vision-language models (LVMs) leads to high computational costs, significant training resources, and deployment challenges.
Existing LVMs require more sophisticated and fine-grained multimodal understanding and reasoning capabilities to address complex cognitive tasks beyond basic recognition.
The challenge of curating high-quality, diverse, and well-aligned multimodal training data essential for effective and generalizable LVMs.
results: SAIL-VL2-2B ranks first among officially released open-source models under the 4B parameter scale on the OpenCompass leaderboard (average score 70.31), and SAIL-VL2-8B-Thinking achieves a new SOTA of 54.4 on OpenCompass multimodal reasoning benchmarks.

Leading performance was achieved across various multimodal understanding tasks (e.g., MMBench-v1.1 86.77 for 2B, DocVQA 95.28 for 8B) and strong results on challenging reasoning benchmarks (e.g., MathVista 75.8 for 8B-Thinking), often outperforming larger models.

Training efficiency was substantially improved through data packing strategies, nearly doubling Streaming Multiprocessor utilization and accelerating training by 50-98%, while also yielding an average +0.7% performance gain.
solution: A comprehensive data curation pipeline (SAIL-Caption2, Synthetic VQA, SAIL-Video, SAIL-Instruction2, Multimodal CoT) using automated quality assessment, filtering, and synthetic generation for hundreds of millions of high-quality samples.

A flexible model architecture comprising an optimized SAIL-ViT vision encoder (including arbitrary-resolution support) and Qwen3 Large Language Models, incorporating sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) designs for computational efficiency.

A multi-stage progressive training framework involving SAIL-ViT optimization, basic and multi-task pre-training, and advanced post-training with Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and Reinforcement Learning (RL) using Chain-of-Thought (LongCoT, Think-Fusion) strategies for complex reasoning.
paper_summary.summary: SAIL-VL2, an open-suite vision-language foundation model from Douyin SAIL Team and LV-NUS Lab, achieves state-of-the-art multimodal understanding and reasoning efficiently at 2B and 8B parameter scales. It leverages an advanced data curation pipeline, innovative architecture including sparse Mixture-of-Experts, and a progressive SFT-RL hybrid training framework to demonstrate strong performance while challenging the trend of solely scaling model size.

title: SAIL-VL2 Technical Report

abstract: Large vision-language models exhibit inherent capabilities to handle diverse visual perception tasks. In this paper, we introduce VisionReasoner, a unified framework capable of reasoning and solving multiple visual perception tasks within a shared model. Specifically, by designing a unified reward mechanism and multi-object cognitive learning strategies, VisionReasoner enhances its reasoning capabilities to analyze visual inputs, and addresses diverse perception tasks within a unified model. VisionReasoner generates a structured reasoning process before delivering the desired outputs responding to user queries. Human evaluation reveals the reasoning process of VisionReasoner is faithful and reliable even without annotated reasoning train data. To rigorously assess unified visual perception capabilities, we evaluate VisionReasoner on ten diverse tasks spanning three critical domains: detection, segmentation, and counting. Experimental results show that VisionReasoner achieves superior performance as a unified model, outperforming the baseline Qwen2.5VL by relative margins of 29.1\% on COCO (detection), 22.1\% on ReasonSeg (segmentation), and 13.2\% on CountBench (counting).

keyInsights: Diverse visual perception tasks can be effectively unified under a 'multi-object cognition' framework, allowing a single, reasoning-integrated model to address them comprehensively.

Reinforcement learning, particularly with a carefully designed unified reward system that balances structural reasoning and prediction accuracy, is an effective strategy for enhancing LVLMs' capabilities in complex visual perception.
Efficient multi-object matching is crucial for the scalability and feasibility of RL training in tasks involving multiple entities, significantly speeding up reward computation.

VisionReasoner can generate highly faithful and consistent reasoning processes that align with both visual content and final outputs, even without explicit human-annotated reasoning data, thereby improving model interpretability.
originalProblem: Existing large vision-language models (LVLMs) and traditional vision methods often rely on fragmented, task-specific modules or fine-tuning for diverse visual perception tasks, limiting their generality.

Previous applications of reinforcement learning (RL) in LVLMs for visual tasks have been largely task-specific, requiring distinct training data and reward designs, which hinders scalability and unification.
LVLMs struggle to handle complex, reasoning-intensive visual instructions, often requiring more than simple pattern recognition to understand nuanced queries.
results: VisionReasoner-7B achieved substantial performance gains over its Qwen2.5VL-7B baseline, with a relative improvement of 29.1% on COCO-val for detection, 22.1% on ReasonSeg-test for segmentation, and 13.2% on CountBench-test for counting.
The efficient multi-object matching pipeline (batch computation + Hungarian algorithm) demonstrated a 4x speedup, reducing matching time from 2x10^-3s to 5x10^-4s in scenarios with 30 objects.

Human evaluation confirmed high consistency of the model's generated reasoning, with 97.0% image consistency and 90.5% answer consistency on ReasonSeg-val, reaching 100% for high-accuracy predictions (IoU > 0.75).

Ablation studies indicated that the non-repeat reward successfully reduced reasoning length and improved performance, and the integrated reasoning module significantly boosted performance on complex reasoning segmentation tasks.
solution: The VisionReasoner framework unifies diverse visual perception tasks (detection, segmentation, counting) within a single LVLM architecture (initialized from Qwen2.5-VL), utilizing a reasoning module and an optional segmentation module.

It employs Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), an on-policy reinforcement learning algorithm, to foster structured reasoning capabilities and optimize for specific visual perception objectives.

A unified reward mechanism is introduced, combining 'format rewards' (for structured and non-repetitive reasoning) and 'accuracy rewards' (for precise multi-object localization based on bounding boxes and center points), to guide RL training across various tasks.

An efficient multi-object matching pipeline, leveraging batch computation and the Hungarian algorithm, is developed to accurately and quickly match predicted objects with ground truths for effective reward calculation during RL training.
paper_summary.summary: VisionReasoner presents a unified framework that integrates reasoning-driven visual perception for diverse tasks like detection, segmentation, and counting within a single model. By leveraging reinforcement learning with a novel reward mechanism, the model achieves state-of-the-art performance on various benchmarks while generating interpretable and consistent reasoning processes.

title: VisionReasoner: Unified Reasoning-Integrated Visual Perception via Reinforcement Learning

abstract: Recent advancements in large reasoning models have fueled growing interest in extending such capabilities to multimodal domains. However, despite notable progress in visual reasoning, the lack of transparent and reproducible data curation and training strategies remains a major barrier to scalable research. In this work, we introduce OpenMMReasoner, a fully transparent two-stage recipe for multimodal reasoning spanning supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and reinforcement learning (RL). In the SFT stage, we construct an 874K-sample cold-start dataset with rigorous step-by-step validation, providing a strong foundation for reasoning capabilities. The subsequent RL stage leverages a 74K-sample dataset across diverse domains to further sharpen and stabilize these abilities, resulting in a more robust and efficient learning process. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that our training recipe not only surpasses strong baselines but also highlights the critical role of data quality and training design in shaping multimodal reasoning performance. Notably, our method achieves a 11.6% improvement over the Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct baseline across nine multimodal reasoning benchmarks, establishing a solid empirical foundation for future large-scale multimodal reasoning research. We open-sourced all our codes, pipeline, and data at this https URL.

keyInsights: Answer diversity in Supervised Fine-tuning (SFT) data, achieved through methods like multi-sampling and cross-domain mixing, critically enhances a model's overall reasoning performance, while aggressive filtering can be detrimental.
Distilling SFT data from a powerful teacher model (e.g., Qwen3-VL-235B-Instruct) significantly improves reasoning capabilities and data efficiency.

The Group Sequence Policy Optimization (GSPO) algorithm offers superior stability and faster convergence in multimodal Reinforcement Learning, and training with RL can lead to beneficial cross-domain transfer of textual reasoning abilities.
originalProblem: Existing Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) often lack transparency and reproducibility in their training pipelines, especially for Reinforcement Learning (RL) components.
Detailed insights into data curation, training strategies, and comprehensive ablation analyses for LMMs are frequently not provided, hindering community understanding and further research.
A gap exists between the advanced capabilities of LMMs and the practical, replicable knowledge required to reproduce and build upon these models.
results: OpenMMReasoner achieved an average accuracy of 54.3% across nine multimodal reasoning benchmarks, setting a new state-of-the-art for open-source models, with specific scores of 79.5% on MathVista and 63.8% on MathVerse.

The model demonstrated enhanced token efficiency, generating accurate responses with significantly fewer tokens compared to prior methods, such as using approximately 9,893 tokens on MathVision where another model used 17,876.
Reinforcement Learning training led to a notable improvement in textual reasoning, with the model's average textual reasoning score reaching 29.4%, a substantial increase from the 15.1% baseline.
solution: A fully transparent, two-stage training recipe combining Supervised Fine-tuning (SFT) and Reinforcement Learning (RL) is developed for Large Multimodal Models, starting with Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct.

The SFT stage employs a meticulous data curation pipeline, including distillation from a powerful teacher model (Qwen3-VL-235B-Instruct), diverse answer sampling (×8), and cross-domain data mixing, without aggressive filtering.
The RL stage leverages Group Sequence Policy Optimization (GSPO) with a composite reward function emphasizing accuracy and an overlength penalty to ensure efficient, concise reasoning.
paper_summary.summary: OpenMMReasoner introduces a transparent, two-stage Supervised Fine-tuning and Reinforcement Learning recipe for multimodal reasoning, establishing a new state-of-the-art for open-source models across nine diverse benchmarks. This approach improves token efficiency and boosts cross-domain textual reasoning capabilities, with all components released as open source.

title: OpenMMReasoner: Pushing the Frontiers for Multimodal Reasoning with an Open and General Recipe

abstract: Visual reasoning, a cornerstone of human intelligence, encompasses complex perceptual and logical processes essential for solving diverse visual problems. While advances in computer vision have produced powerful models for various perceptual tasks, leveraging these for general visual reasoning remains challenging. Prior work demonstrates that augmenting LLMs with vision models via supervised finetuning improves performance, but faces key limitations such as expensive data generation, reliance on careful data filtering, and poor generalization. To address these issues, we propose ReVPT to enhance multi-modal LLMs' abilities to reason about and use visual tools through reinforcement learning. We introduce a novel RL algorithm based on GRPO, designed to train models to reason with a suite of four visual tools. Through extensive experiments, we show that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance on several perception-heavy benchmarks, including SAT, CV-Bench, BLINK and MMStar, significantly outperforming the supervised and text-based RL finetuning baselines. Notably, Our ReVPT-3B and ReVPT-7B outperform the instruct models by 9.03% and 9.44% on CV-Bench. Finally, we bring to the community new insights on RL-based visual tool-usage through extensive ablations. Our code is available at this https URL.

organization_info.name: Huazhong University of Science and Technology

keyInsights: Reinforcement learning, particularly with the GRPO algorithm, offers a more adaptive and scalable approach to training MLLMs for visual tool use compared to supervised finetuning.

A "cold-start" phase, involving initial SFT on synthetically generated tool-use data, is crucial for preventing MLLMs from neglecting visual tools and for preserving their general capabilities during RL training.
ReV PT learns to optimize the strategic selection of visual tools, resulting in higher accuracy when tools are used, even if the overall frequency of tool calls decreases after RL optimization.
originalProblem: Bridging the gap between verbal reasoning in large language models (LLMs) and specialized computer vision (CV) for robust, general visual reasoning remains challenging.

Prior tool-augmented MLLMs primarily relied on supervised finetuning (SFT), which is costly for data curation, requires extensive data filtering, and limits generalization to novel tool-use scenarios.
MLLMs often struggle with complex visual perception tasks requiring granular analysis, such as spatial relationships, object counting, and depth estimation.
results: ReV PT achieves state-of-the-art performance on several perception-intensive benchmarks, including MMVP, MMStar, CV-Bench, BLINK, and BLINK-Hard, with an average improvement of over 5% compared to instruct-tuned base models.

The method outperforms SFT baselines and text-based RL agents, and surpasses commercial models like GPT-4.1 and Gemini-2.0-Flash on challenging visual reasoning subsets (e.g., BLINK-Depth and Relation).

Ablation studies confirm that both the cold-start process and the integration of specific tools like object detection and depth estimation are critical for the framework's effectiveness, with their removal leading to significant performance degradation.
solution: ReV PT introduces a two-stage reinforcement learning (RL) framework, built upon Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), to teach MLLMs adaptive visual tool usage.

A "cold-start" phase leverages GPT-4.1 to synthesize high-quality, tool-augmented reasoning data, followed by SFT on this data, to establish a robust initial policy for sequential vision-tool invocation.

The system integrates four perception-oriented visual tools (Object Detection, Zoom In, Edge Detection, Depth Estimation) and a simple binary reward function to guide RL training and optimize tool selection.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from ONE Lab, HUST, University of Washington, and Zhejiang University developed Reinforced Visual Perception with Tools (ReV PT), a framework that trains multimodal language models (MLLMs) to adaptively use visual tools via reinforcement learning. This method achieves state-of-the-art performance on perception-heavy visual reasoning benchmarks by learning to strategically select and interpret tool outputs, overcoming limitations of supervised finetuning approaches.

title: Reinforced Visual Perception with Tools

abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) provides a principled framework for improving Vision-Language Models (VLMs) on complex reasoning tasks. However, existing RL approaches often rely on human-annotated labels or task-specific heuristics to define verifiable rewards, both of which are costly and difficult to scale. We introduce VisPlay, a self-evolving RL framework that enables VLMs to autonomously improve their reasoning abilities using large amounts of unlabeled image data. Starting from a single base VLM, VisPlay assigns the model into two interacting roles: an Image-Conditioned Questioner that formulates challenging yet answerable visual questions, and a Multimodal Reasoner that generates silver responses. These roles are jointly trained with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), which incorporates diversity and difficulty rewards to balance the complexity of generated questions with the quality of the silver answers. VisPlay scales efficiently across two model families. When trained on Qwen2.5-VL and MiMo-VL, VisPlay achieves consistent improvements in visual reasoning, compositional generalization, and hallucination reduction across eight benchmarks, including MM-Vet and MMMU, demonstrating a scalable path toward self-evolving multimodal intelligence. The project page is available at this https URL

keyInsights: The self-evolving paradigm, successful in LLMs, can be effectively adapted to Vision-Language Models by establishing a closed-loop self-play system with internal reward mechanisms.
Designing an Image-Conditioned Questioner that generates tasks based on the Reasoner's uncertainty and diversity can autonomously drive progressive learning without human supervision.
Filtering self-generated question-answer pairs to include only those with moderate confidence allows the Reasoner to learn from challenging yet attainable examples, improving overall robustness.
originalProblem: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) heavily depend on costly, labor-intensive human-annotated datasets, limiting their scalability and continuous improvement.
The successful self-evolving paradigm observed in Large Language Models (LLMs) has not been widely adapted to VLMs due to the complexities of integrating visual modalities.
Existing reinforcement learning methods for VLMs typically require external, verifiable rewards derived from human annotations or specific heuristics, reintroducing scalability issues.
results: Demonstrated consistent overall performance improvements across various VLM architectures, with Qwen2.5-VL-3B-Instruct's average score increasing from 30.61% to 47.27% over three iterations.
Achieved a substantial reduction in visual hallucination, improving the hallucination score for Qwen2.5-VL-3B-Instruct from 32.81% to 94.95% by the second iteration.
Showed competitive average accuracy compared to models trained on human-curated data, validating the quality and effectiveness of self-generated supervision for VLM enhancement.
solution: Introduces VisPlay, a self-evolving reinforcement learning framework that operates a single VLM in a closed-loop, self-play system using only unlabeled images.
Decomposes the VLM into two interacting roles: an Image-Conditioned Questioner (generating diverse, challenging questions) and a Multimodal Reasoner (answering these questions).
Employs Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with internal uncertainty and diversity rewards to train the Questioner, and uses filtered, moderately confident pseudo-labels to train the Reasoner.
paper_summary.summary: The VisPlay framework enables Vision-Language Models (VLMs) to autonomously enhance their reasoning and factual grounding by learning from unlabeled image data, eliminating the need for human annotations. This approach achieves consistent performance gains across diverse visual reasoning tasks, including reducing hallucination for Qwen2.5-VL-3B-Instruct from 32.81% to 94.95%, and demonstrates competitive average accuracy compared to models trained with human-curated data.

title: VisPlay: Self-Evolving Vision-Language Models from Images

abstract: We introduce Skywork-R1V3, an advanced, open-source vision-language model (VLM) that pioneers a new approach to visual reasoning. Its key innovation lies in effectively transferring reasoning skills from text-only Large Language Models (LLMs) to visual tasks. The strong performance of Skywork-R1V3 primarily stems from our elaborate post-training RL framework, which effectively activates and enhances the model's reasoning ability, without the need for additional continue pre-training. Through this framework, we further uncover the fundamental role of the connector module in achieving robust cross-modal alignment for multimodal reasoning models. In addition, we introduce a unique indicator of reasoning capability, the entropy of critical reasoning tokens, which has proven highly effective for checkpoint selection during RL training. Skywork-R1V3 achieves state-of-the-art results on MMMU, significantly improving from 64.3% to 76.0%. This performance matches entry-level human capabilities. Remarkably, our RL-powered post-training approach enables even the 38B parameter model to rival top closed-source VLMs. The implementation successfully transfers mathematical reasoning to other subject-related reasoning tasks. We also include an analysis of curriculum learning and reinforcement finetuning strategies, along with a broader discussion on multimodal reasoning. Skywork-R1V3 represents a significant leap in multimodal reasoning, showcasing RL as a powerful engine for advancing open-source VLM capabilities.

keyInsights: Reinforcement learning, particularly PPO and GRPO, effectively activates and transfers complex reasoning abilities to VLMs, leading to generalizable skills without extensive pre-training.
The cross-modal connector module is fundamental for stable RL training and robust cross-modal alignment, with connector-only tuning proving effective for rebalancing domain knowledge post-RL.
The "entropy of critical reasoning tokens" is a novel and effective indicator for genuine reasoning capability, useful for checkpoint selection during RL training.
originalProblem: Open-source Vision-Language Models (VLMs) often lack human-level generality and robust performance in complex visual reasoning tasks.
A significant performance disparity exists between proprietary VLMs and open-source alternatives, particularly in structured visual thinking and deep reasoning.
Transferring sophisticated reasoning skills from text-only Large Language Models (LLMs) to visual tasks in VLMs is challenging, often requiring extensive pre-training.
results: Achieves state-of-the-art performance among open-source models, scoring 76.0% on MMMU and 78.5% on MMK12, demonstrating capabilities matching entry-level human experts.
Consistently outperforms larger open-source models and several proprietary models on mathematics benchmarks, including 77.1% on MathVista and a notable 142/150 on the 2025 GAOKAO math exam.

The model exhibits a "test-time scaling law" where reasoning accuracy improves with increased thinking token budgets during inference, achieving 76.0% accuracy at 16K tokens, and can adapt its inference style (e.g., "Slow Thinking" vs. "NoWait").
solution: Employs a three-stage post-training pipeline based on the InternVL-38B model, primarily leveraging Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) and Group-normalized Reward Policy Optimization (GRPO).
Utilizes a composite reward function that prioritizes accuracy while also rewarding adherence to a structured "think before answering" reasoning format.

Integrates cold-start finetuning with high-quality Chain-of-Thought (CoT) data, followed by reinforcement finetuning, and concludes with connector-only tuning for rebalancing the knowledge base across diverse domains.
paper_summary.summary: Skywork-R1V3, an open-source 38B parameter vision-language model by Skywork AI, Kunlun Inc., employs an innovative reinforcement learning-powered post-training approach to achieve visual reasoning capabilities competitive with leading proprietary models. It demonstrates scores of 76.0% on MMMU and 142/150 on the 2025 GAOKAO math exam, showcasing the transfer of mathematical reasoning skills to broader scientific tasks.

title: Skywork-R1V3 Technical Report

abstract:
Vision-language models (VLMs) trained via reinforcement learning with
verifiable reward (RLVR) have shown notable progress in scaling test-time
compute effectively. In this work, we investigate how synthesized RL data can
further improve RLVR. To this end, we propose \textbf{SynthRL}-a scalable and
guaranteed pipeline for automatic data scaling in reasoning-oriented RL
training. SynthRL comprises three key stages: (1) selecting seed questions with
appropriate distribution, (2) augmenting them into more challenging variants
while preserving the original answers, and (3) a guaranteed verification stage
that ensures near-perfect correctness and difficulty enhancement. Our empirical
experiments demonstrate SynthRL's scalability and effectiveness. When applied
to the MMK12 dataset, SynthRL synthesizes over 3.3K additional verifiable,
challenging questions from approximately 8K seed samples. Models trained with
our synthesized data achieve consistent gains across five out-of-domain visual
math reasoning benchmarks, with a significant improvement over baseline models
trained on seed data alone. Notably, detailed analysis reveals that the gains
are more pronounced on the most challenging evaluation samples, highlighting
SynthRL's effectiveness in eliciting deeper and more complex reasoning
patterns.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

keyInsights: More challenging yet still correct training samples provide richer learning signals for RLVR than volume increases alone
Verification alignment with target model capabilities is crucial - using non-target verifiers reduces performance by 1.5%
Benefits scale with seed dataset size, widening from +0.2% at 2K samples to +1.0% at 8K samples, indicating complementary value to traditional scaling
originalProblem: RLVR shows promise for enhancing VLM reasoning but faces data scalability challenges, as existing selection methods are constrained by original dataset volume and bias
Creating challenging yet verifiable training data for visual reasoning requires expensive human annotation and step-by-step reasoning supervision
Traditional data augmentation lacks correctness guarantees and difficulty calibration needed for effective RL training where unreliable rewards lead to suboptimal policies
results: Generated 3,380 verified challenging questions from 8,072 seeds, achieving 58.0% average accuracy versus 57.0% baseline across five benchmarks
Largest improvements on medium (+1.7%) and hard (+1.6%) evaluation samples while showing slight decrease (-0.5%) on easy samples, confirming targeted enhancement of complex reasoning
Ablation studies confirm necessity of verification (1.4% drop without), multi-pass evaluation (0.7% drop with single-pass), and augmentation over replacement strategy (1.1% performance difference)
solution: Three-stage automated pipeline: difficulty-based seed selection using Monte Carlo rollouts to identify easy questions, synthesis using powerful VLMs prompted to create harder variants without seeing answers, and guaranteed verification ensuring correctness and increased difficulty
Target model-aligned verification using rollout pass counts with dual criteria: correctness (C_cand ≥ 4) and difficulty enhancement (C_cand ≤ C_ori - 2)
Augmentation strategy combining original and synthesized data rather than replacement to create richer training distributions
paper_summary.summary: NUS-TRAIL researchers develop SynthRL, an automated three-stage pipeline that synthesizes more challenging visual reasoning problems while preserving original answers to enhance reinforcement learning training for vision-language models, generating 3,380 verified harder questions from 8,072 MMK12 seed samples through difficulty-based selection, Gemini-2.5-Flash synthesis, and guaranteed verification using Monte Carlo rollouts, achieving consistent +1.0% average accuracy gains across five out-of-domain benchmarks (MathVerse, MathVision, MathVista, WeMath, DynaMath) with pronounced improvements on medium (+1.7%) and hard (+1.6%) evaluation samples, demonstrating that strategically augmenting training distributions with verifiably correct yet more complex variants provides richer learning signals for RLVR than traditional data scaling approaches.

title: SynthRL: Scaling Visual Reasoning with Verifiable Data Synthesis

abstract: The remarkable reasoning capability of large language models (LLMs) stems from cognitive behaviors that emerge through reinforcement with verifiable rewards. This work investigates how to transfer this principle to Multimodal LLMs (MLLMs) to unlock advanced visual reasoning. We introduce a two-stage paradigm built on Qwen2.5-VL-7B: a massive linguistic cold-start fine-tuning, followed by multimodal reinforcement learning (RL) spanning nearly 1,000 steps, surpassing all previous open-source efforts in scale. This pioneering work reveals three fundamental insights: 1) Behavior transfer emerges surprisingly early in cold start due to linguistic mental imagery. 2) Cold start broadly memorizes visual behaviors, while RL critically discerns and scales up effective patterns. 3) Transfer strategically favors high-utility behaviors such as visual reflection. Our resulting model, Open-Vision-Reasoner (OVR), achieves state-of-the-art performance on a suite of reasoning benchmarks, including 95.3% on MATH500, 51.8% on MathVision and 54.6% on MathVerse. We release our model, data, and training dynamics to catalyze the development of more capable, behavior-aligned multimodal reasoners.

keyInsights: Visual-specific cognitive behaviors, such as visual reflection, emerge remarkably early in the linguistic cold-start phase due to 'mental imagery' encoded in linguistic patterns.

The linguistic cold-start phase broadly familiarizes the model with diverse cognitive behaviors, while large-scale multimodal RL critically discerns and strategically amplifies only the most effective ones for generalization.

The transfer of cognitive behaviors is strategic, with behaviors like backtracking showing significant growth in transfer rate (from 2.5% to 17.3%) due to their higher utility in complex visual reasoning.
originalProblem: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) often lack the advanced reasoning capabilities (e.g., backtracking, self-verification) seen in Large Language Models (LLMs).
Previous MLLM reinforcement learning efforts predominantly used RLHF, which is susceptible to 'reward hacking,' unlike the more robust Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Reward (RLVR) paradigm.
A systematic understanding of how linguistic cognitive behaviors from LLMs transfer to MLLMs for complex visual reasoning tasks was missing.
results: OVR-7B achieved state-of-the-art visual reasoning among 7B models, scoring 51.8% on MathVision, 54.6% on MathVerse, and 33.5% on DynaMath, demonstrating successful cross-modal transfer.
The model exhibited enhanced language reasoning, outperforming other 7B open-source models by over 10% on challenging math reasoning tasks like AIME 2024 (63.5%) and AIME 2025 (52.1%).

Linguistic cold-start initially impaired visual perception but long-term multimodal RL effectively recovered and enhanced perceptual performance, while RL alone showed limitations for pure perceptual tasks.
solution: A two-stage training paradigm was developed using Qwen2.5-VL-7B, starting with a 'linguistic cold start' fine-tuning on over 2 million language-only reasoning examples.
The linguistic cold start was followed by large-scale multimodal Reinforcement Learning (RL) using the Open-Reasoner-Zero framework on over 0.3 million mixed-modality examples.
A minimalist, rule-based binary reward function was employed during RL, providing a clear and unhackable signal for correct answers, aligning with the RLVR paradigm.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers developed Open-Vision-Reasoner (OVR), an MLLM that transfers linguistic cognitive behaviors to the visual domain, achieving state-of-the-art results on visual reasoning benchmarks such as 51.8% on MathVision and 54.6% on MathVerse for 7B models.
